topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly against the mouse. The headline screamed my name, the content was undeniably mine—my meticulously researched piece on urban planning. But the byline? It was a stranger’s. A name I didn't recognize, a name associated with a completely different publication. A slow, burning resentment began to bloom in my chest, hot and suffocating. I reread the attribution, the discrepancy glaringly obvious. I stood up abruptly, pacing the cramped office, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I needed to confront someone, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a dry, brittle fury.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the pixels blurring into meaningless shapes. It was an insult, a calculated slight delivered with surgical precision. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I felt a sudden, sharp need to slam my fist onto the desk, to shatter something tangible. Who had done this? The thought spun in my mind, a vicious, churning vortex. I clenched my hands into fists, the knuckles turning white against my skin. The silence of the room amplified the drumming in my ears, each beat echoing the rising tide of indignation. I didn't type a reply; I just closed the laptop with a decisive, jarring snap, the sound echoing the finality of my growing anger.

[story 3]

When I saw the published article, the color drained from my face. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a deliberate erasure. A deep, icy chill settled over my skin, making my limbs feel heavy and unresponsive. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a profound sense of being undermined, exposed. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my neck. Every carefully constructed thought dissolved into a single, sharp demand: accountability. I turned back to the computer, my movements stiff, deliberate. I didn't weep or shout. Instead, I began typing, each character a deliberate, sharp strike against the keyboard, fueled by a simmering, controlled rage that promised a reckoning.
